title: Audit all Company record actions
user_story: As a Platform Admin, I want the platform to automatically record every create, update, delete, and status-change action performed on Company records, so that all changes to this mandatory audit entity are fully traceable.
acceptance_criteria: Given a Company record is created, updated, deleted, or has its status changed, when the action is committed, then an audit entry is automatically generated for that action.; Given any Company-level change, when it occurs, then the change is not persisted without a corresponding audit entry (audit is mandatory, not optional).; Given a Company status-change action, when it occurs, then the action type is captured distinctly as a status-change in the audit trail.; Given a Company action is initiated but fails or is rolled back, when the transaction does not commit, then no misleading audit entry is left representing a change that did not take effect.

title: Audit all Employee record actions
user_story: As a Platform Admin, I want the platform to automatically record every create, update, delete, and status-change action performed on Employee records, so that all changes to this mandatory audit entity are fully accountable.
acceptance_criteria: Given an Employee record is created, updated, deleted, or has its status changed, when the action is committed, then an audit entry is automatically generated for that action.; Given an Employee status change (e.g. active to inactive, onboarding to active), when it occurs, then the action type is captured as a status-change in the audit trail.; Given any Employee-level change, when it occurs, then the change is not persisted without a corresponding audit entry.; Given changes to an Employee (Non-User) who has no system login, when those changes are made by an administrator, then the action is still audited identically to any other Employee record change.

title: Capture complete audit data fields
user_story: As a Company Admin, I want each audit entry to capture the entity type, record identifier, and affected field/attribute, so that I can pinpoint exactly what was changed and where.
acceptance_criteria: Given an audited action occurs, when the audit entry is written, then it records the entity type and the record identifier of the affected record.; Given a field-level change occurs, when the audit entry is written, then it records the specific field/attribute that was affected.; Given a single change touches multiple fields, when the audit entry is written, then each changed field/attribute is individually represented so no modified attribute is omitted.; Given a create or delete action (no single field), when the audit entry is written, then the entity type, record identifier, and action type still uniquely identify the affected record.
source: FR 6.29.2 (Audit Data — entity type, record identifier, field/attribute)

title: Capture before-and-after values
user_story: As a Company Admin, I want each audit entry to record the previous value and the new value for a changed field, so that I can see the exact transition that took place.
acceptance_criteria: Given a field value is updated, when the audit entry is written, then it stores both the previous value and the new value for that field.; Given a record is created, when the audit entry is written, then the previous value reflects that no prior value existed while the new value captures the initial value.; Given a record or field is deleted/cleared, when the audit entry is written, then the previous value is retained and the new value reflects the empty/deleted state.; Given multiple fields change in one action, when the audit entry is written, then each field carries its own previous and new value pair.

title: Capture actor, timestamp, and action type
user_story: As a Company Admin, I want each audit entry to record the timestamp, the user/actor responsible, and the action type, so that I can attribute every change to a who, when, and what.
acceptance_criteria: Given an audited action occurs, when the audit entry is written, then it records the timestamp of the action, the user/actor who performed it, and the action type (create, update, delete, or status-change).; Given an action performed by an automated process or system job, when the audit entry is written, then the actor is still identifiable (e.g. system/service identity) rather than blank.; Given the audit timestamp is recorded, when it is stored, then it is precise enough to establish chronological ordering of successive changes to the same record.
source: FR 6.29.2 (Audit Data — timestamp, user/actor, action type)

title: Active audit retention for one year
user_story: As a Platform Admin, I want audit records retained in active storage for one year, so that recent change history is readily available for review.
acceptance_criteria: Given an audit entry is created, when one year has not yet elapsed, then the entry remains available in active retention.; Given an audit entry, when it is queried within the one-year active retention window, then it is returned without needing archival access.; Given an audit entry approaches the end of its one-year active window, when the window elapses, then the entry transitions to archival rather than becoming unavailable.

title: Archive audit records for seven years total
user_story: As a Platform Admin, I want audit records archived so that the total retention reaches seven years, so that the platform meets long-term compliance and audit obligations.
acceptance_criteria: Given an audit entry has exceeded the one-year active window, when retention is processed, then the entry is moved to archival storage rather than deleted.; Given an archived audit entry, when it is within the seven-year total retention period, then it remains retrievable from archival storage.; Given an archived audit entry, when it is older than seven years total, then it becomes eligible for disposal.; Given the archival transition and disposal processes run, when they execute, then they themselves preserve the tamper-resistance and integrity of the retained records.

title: View record-level change history
user_story: As a Company Admin, I want an in-application utility to view the chronological change history for a specific record, so that I can review how that record evolved over time without leaving the application.
acceptance_criteria: Given I am an authorized user viewing a specific record, when I open the change-history utility, then I see its changes listed in chronological order.; Given a record's history is displayed, when I inspect an entry, then I can see the field changed, the previous and new values, the timestamp, and the actor.; Given I am not authorized to view the record's history, when I attempt to open it, then access is denied.; Given a record has history spanning both active and archived storage, when I view it, then the utility presents the available chronological history consistently to the authorized user.

title: Secure, tamper-resistant, tenant-isolated logs
user_story: As a Platform Admin, I want audit logs to be tamper-resistant with role-based access and tenant boundary enforcement, so that audit data remains trustworthy and isolated per tenant.
acceptance_criteria: Given an audit log entry exists, when any actor attempts to modify or delete it, then the log resists tampering and the attempt is prevented (and ideally itself recorded).; Given a user requests audit data, when access is evaluated, then it is granted only per their role-based permissions.; Given a user belongs to one tenant, when they query audit logs or record history, then they can only access audit data within their own tenant boundary and never another tenant's data.; Given tamper-resistance and RBAC controls are in place, when audit data is retained across active and archival storage, then these protections apply uniformly across both.

title: View audit history within portfolio scope
user_story: As a Portfolio Admin, I want to view the chronological change history of Company and Employee records within my portfolio, so that I can oversee changes across the group companies I am responsible for while staying within my tenant boundary.
acceptance_criteria: Given I am a Portfolio Admin, when I open the record-level change-history utility for a Company or Employee within my portfolio, then I see its changes in chronological order with field, previous/new values, timestamp, and actor.; Given a record belongs to a portfolio or tenant outside my scope, when I attempt to view its audit history, then access is denied by tenant boundary and role-based access enforcement.; Given my role-based permissions, when audit data is presented, then I only see audit entries I am authorized to view.
source: FR 6.29.4, FR 6.29.5 (Record-Level History + RBAC/tenant boundary — Portfolio Admin)

title: View audit history within group company scope
user_story: As a Group Company Admin, I want to view the chronological change history of Company and Employee records within my group company, so that I can investigate changes across the companies I manage without crossing tenant boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Given I am a Group Company Admin, when I open the record-level change-history utility for a Company or Employee within my group company, then I see its changes in chronological order with field, previous/new values, timestamp, and actor.; Given a record belongs to a company outside my group company or another tenant, when I attempt to view its audit history, then access is denied by role-based access and tenant boundary enforcement.; Given multiple companies under my group company, when I review audit data, then each entry is correctly attributed to its originating company.
source: FR 6.29.4, FR 6.29.5 (Record-Level History + RBAC/tenant boundary — Group Company Admin)

title: View my own record change history
user_story: As an Employee (User), I want to view the chronological change history of my own employee record where I am authorized, so that I can see what changed on my profile, when, and by whom.
acceptance_criteria: Given I am an authorized Employee (User), when I open the change-history utility for my own employee record, then I see its changes in chronological order with field, previous/new values, timestamp, and actor.; Given I attempt to view another employee's record history, when access is evaluated, then it is denied unless my role explicitly authorizes it.; Given I am viewing my own record history, when the data is displayed, then it is limited to my record within my tenant boundary.
source: FR 6.29.4, FR 6.29.5 (Record-Level History self-service — Employee User)

title: Audit changes to non-user employee records
user_story: As an Employee (Non-User) whose data is maintained on my behalf, I want every change to my employee record to be audited even though I have no system login, so that my record remains accountable and reviewable by authorized administrators.
acceptance_criteria: Given an Employee (Non-User) record is created, updated, deleted, or has its status changed by an administrator, when the action is committed, then a complete audit entry (entity type, record id, field, previous/new value, timestamp, actor, action type) is generated.; Given the Employee (Non-User) has no login, when changes are made to their record, then the actor recorded is the administrator or process that performed the change.; Given an authorized administrator reviews the non-user employee's record, when they open the change-history utility, then the full chronological history is available within tenant boundary and role-based access rules.
source: FR 6.29.1, FR 6.29.2, FR 6.29.4 (Audit coverage for Employee Non-User records)

title: Deny unauthorized access to audit data
user_story: As a Company Admin, I want role-based and tenant-scoped access strictly enforced on all audit data and record history, so that only authorized users within the correct tenant can view audit information.
acceptance_criteria: Given a user without audit-view permission, when they attempt to access audit logs or record-level history, then access is denied.; Given a user from a different tenant, when they attempt to query audit data belonging to my company, then the tenant boundary blocks access and no data is returned.; Given an authorized Company Admin, when they access audit data, then they can only see audit entries for records within their own company and tenant.; Given any denied access attempt, when it occurs, then it does not reveal the existence or content of the protected audit data.
source: FR 6.29.5 (Security — RBAC and tenant boundary enforcement)
